skill.name: azure-resource-lookup
skill.description: Find, list, and summarize Azure resources across subscriptions and resource groups using Azure Resource Graph and supported Azure MCP tools. Use it for read-only inventory, tag checks, orphaned resource discovery, and resource state reviews.
skill.summary: Find and summarize Azure resources with read-only Azure Resource Graph queries.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk alerts, but the Ruby shell execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown and KQL examples. The remaining real concern is that the skill guides Azure CLI command generation and execution for read-only Azure Resource Graph queries. No prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, mutation workflow, or credential harvesting evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Azure CLI Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to generate and run Azure CLI Resource Graph queries. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but it can expose Azure inventory metadata to the assistant session and should be limited to authorized read-only scopes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly directs use of extension_cli_generate and az graph query. The behavior is legitimate for Azure inventory work, but command execution creates a real operational risk factor.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported many Ruby shell backtick findings, but the reviewed locations are Markdown inline code, tables, and fenced examples. No Ruby source file or executable backtick expression was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation for Azure CLI flags and examples. There is no evidence of Ruby execution syntax in an executable file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are KQL Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis marked weak cryptographic algorithm blockers at KQL query lines. The reviewed lines contain Resource Graph summarize, order, and project clauses, not hashing or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are KQL inventory and Advisor projection examples. No weak hash function, encryption routine, or password handling is present.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Error Handling Text
low_findings.description: The static system reconnaissance finding points to guidance for resolving a BadRequest KQL error. It does not instruct host reconnaissance or collection of local system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited table explains Azure query errors and subscription scoping. No local host probing, privilege discovery, or unrelated reconnaissance behavior appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs assistants to generate and run Azure CLI Resource Graph queries. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but it can expose Azure inventory metadata to the assistant session and should be limited to authorized read-only scopes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly directs use of extension_cli_generate and az graph query. The behavior is legitimate for Azure inventory work, but command execution creates a real operational risk factor.

content.value_statement: Azure teams need fast answers about resources, tags, locations, and ownership across large tenants. This skill guides read-only Azure Resource Graph queries and formats results for clear inventory review.
actual_capabilities: Generates read-only Azure Resource Graph query intents for resource inventory tasks.
Lists resources by type, subscription, resource group, location, and tag status.
Helps find orphaned disks, unused public IP addresses, and detached network interfaces.
Suggests KQL patterns for health, provisioning state, and service-specific discovery.
Routes common single-resource requests toward dedicated Azure MCP tools when available.
limitations: Requires Azure access and appropriate read permissions in the target subscription.
Does not deploy, modify, delete, or configure Azure resources.
Azure Resource Graph results can lag behind real-time resource state.
Large tenants require scoped subscriptions or result limits for practical output.

use_cases.title: Tenant Resource Inventory
use_cases.description: Create a readable inventory of Azure resources across subscriptions, grouped by type, location, or resource group.

use_cases.title: Tag Compliance Review
use_cases.description: Find resources missing required tags and summarize coverage by resource type for governance follow-up.

use_cases.title: Orphaned Resource Discovery
use_cases.description: Identify unattached disks, idle public IP addresses, and unused network interfaces before cleanup planning.

prompt_templates.title: List My Azure Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: List my Azure resources across the current subscription. Show name, type, resource group, and location.

prompt_templates.title: Find Resources By Tag
prompt_templates.prompt: Find Azure resources missing the Environment or CostCenter tag. Group the results by resource type.

prompt_templates.title: Review Orphaned Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Find unattached managed disks, unused public IP addresses, and detached network interfaces. Limit results to 100.

prompt_templates.title: Build Cross-Subscription Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cross-subscription Azure Resource Graph query that counts resources by subscription name, resource type, and location.

output_examples.input: List all web apps in my Azure subscriptions.
output_examples.output: A concise table of App Service resources with name, kind, state, plan, location, and resource group.

output_examples.input: Which resources are missing required governance tags?
output_examples.output: A grouped summary of untagged resources, followed by the highest-impact resource groups to review first.
A filtered list of public IP resources with no attached IP configuration, including location and resource group.

best_practices: Scope queries with subscriptions or result limits before running them in large tenants.
Use dedicated Azure MCP tools when they fully support the requested resource type.
Treat inventory results as sensitive operational data and share them only with authorized users.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to deploy, modify, or delete Azure resources.
Do not run broad tenant-wide queries when a subscription or resource group scope is known.
Do not treat Azure Resource Graph results as real-time monitoring data.

faq.question: Does this skill change Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for read-only lookup, inventory, and discovery workflows.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: The user needs read access, such as Reader role, for the subscriptions or resource groups being queried.

faq.question: Can it query multiple subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide Azure Resource Graph queries that span multiple authorized subscriptions.

faq.question: Does it replace dedicated Azure tools?
faq.answer: No. It recommends dedicated MCP tools first when they fully support the requested resource type.

faq.question: Are results always current?
faq.answer: No. Azure Resource Graph is near real-time, so recent changes may take time to appear.

faq.question: Can it help with governance checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for missing tags, public access settings, health status, and provisioning state.
